Now that we've defined the principle of social proof as the tendency to follow how others are responding in a situation, and we've recognized that the principle is activated in people when they're exposed to evidence of others' responses, it's time to list the three factors that amplify the impact of social proof evidence. As a rule, social proof evidence will be more effective if those exposed to the information — first, perceive that it comes from many others, the more the better. Second, if they see that it comes from others who are similar to them. And third, if they, the observers, are uncertain of how best to respond in the situation. So the principle of social proof is amplified when it involves many others, similar others, and an uncertain situation. The last of these factors' uncertainty makes people want to reduce the doubt, whereas the first two give them a way to do that by simply following what multiple comparable others are doing.
